scan_notes: Top-level rules/, spec/, architecture/, design/, src/, and infra/ directories were not present during this audit; canonical material lives under docs/, engine/, server/, client/, shared/, and scripts/.

verification_runs.command: rtk pnpm --filter @phalanxduel/engine test -- replay.test.ts pass-rules.test.ts facecard.test.ts fsm-trace-fixtures.test.ts
verification_runs.result: passed; Vitest executed 9 engine test files / 87 tests

documentation_inventory.title: Phalanx Duel — Canonical Rules Specification v1.0 (Final)
HTTP and WebSocket Surface Diagram Source
Phalanx Duel — Ranked Play & ELO Roadmap
RISKS.md — Known Risks and Operational Hazards
Canonical state machine definition for Phalanx Duel
PHX-FACECARD-001: Classic Face Card Destruction Eligibility
PHX-TXLOG-003: Game is replayable from initial config + ordered actions

duplicate_rule_sources.notes: Re-states lifecycle, hashing, and replay concepts at a system level.
Encodes phases, match params, event payloads, and turn-result contracts in schemas.
Defines transition authority with human-readable descriptions aligned to rules.
Restates face-card eligibility rules in tests.
Condenses gameplay terms for UI help, but uses different vocabulary than the canonical rules.

ambiguous_rules.summary: Section 13 says destroyed cards go to the graveyard in LIFO order, but the rules do not define whether discard order is semantically observable or just descriptive.
Sections 17 and 20.2 mix span/event language with transaction-hash language without binding one canonical payload shape.

missing_rule_sections.summary: The rules document does not contain a dedicated canonical win-conditions section for LP depletion, card depletion, forfeit, and pass-limit victory even though the review spec requires one and the engine implements them.

mapped_rules.rule_section: 8-12. Attack Resolution, Suit Boundaries, Classic Aces, Classic Face Cards, Damage Persistence

rules_without_engine_mapping.gap: No engine or server logic references modeSpecialStart beyond default schema/state values.
Runtime does not emit the documented PhalanxEvent hierarchy; it records transactionLog entries and optional phaseTrace instead.
The documented EventLog/TurnHash payload does not map to the actual schema/runtime payload.
checkVictory implements LP depletion, card depletion, and pass-limit victory, but the canonical rules do not surface them as a normative section.

engine_logic_without_rule_reference.logic: Partial match parameter normalization clamps maxHandSize to columns and silently recomputes initialDraw.
engine_logic_without_rule_reference.note: Operationally important behavior, but not traced back from docs/RULES.md or docs/system/ARCHITECTURE.md.

engine_logic_without_rule_reference.logic: system:init transitions are recorded with details.type = "pass".
engine_logic_without_rule_reference.note: This internal encoding is not documented and weakens trace clarity for transaction log readers.

engine_logic_without_rule_reference.logic: The replay route returns valid/actionCount/finalStateHash without comparing replayed results to persisted transaction hashes or stored final state.
engine_logic_without_rule_reference.note: Documentation uses stronger replay-verification language than the route currently provides.

event_docs.coverage: Span hierarchy, event envelope, system_error semantics, replay/hash model.
Event-sourcing overview, transactionLog structure, phaseTrace hook.

missing_event_topics: How PhalanxEvent objects are actually generated at runtime; no emitter implementation was found.
How event ids and parentId values are assigned deterministically.
How the documented event stream relates to transactionLog and phaseTrace.
Whether replay reconstruction is event-driven, action-history-driven, or transaction-log-driven; runtime replay uses actionHistory.
What payload shape clients should trust; server broadcasts events: [] while clients consume transactionLog.
How system_error and special_start_window_closed events are emitted; no producers were found.
How phaseTraceDigest is produced or verified for official profiles; only the schema hook exists.
event_model_verification.replay_explanation_quality: Partial. The rules and architecture explain transaction hashing and phase traces well enough to understand deterministic replay, but they do not describe the actual runtime authority boundary: replay currently re-executes actionHistory, emitted PhalanxEvent objects are absent, and the replay route does not validate persisted hashes against the stored match state.

architecture_documents.coverage: Monorepo packages, local URLs, runtime prerequisites.
Server authority, package boundaries, data flow, deterministic replay narrative.
Client screen transitions, HTTP/WS surface, discoverability.

missing_topics.summary: Persistence authority is under-documented. The architecture explains broadcasts and hashing, but not the authoritative role of MatchRepository/state/actionHistory/transactionLog persistence.
Replay validation semantics are under-specified. The docs imply verification, but the route only replays actionHistory and returns a hash/result status.
The relationship between events, transactionLog, phaseTrace, and client narration is not described as a single coherent architecture.
Optional modes called out in the rules, especially SpecialStart and future official verification profiles, are not reflected in the architecture narrative.

rule_vs_code_mismatches.summary: Card ID format drift: the canonical rules document a 5-part card id, while the engine emits a 6-part id with an extra draw index suffix.
Pass-limit threshold drift: the rules say a player forfeits when passes are greater than the configured limits, but the engine ends the game when passes are greater than or equal to the limit.
Special Start Mode is specified, including a non-pass window and special_start_window_closed event, but no runtime implementation was found beyond schema/default fields.
Card Scarcity Invariant drift: the rules say configurations are invalid if initialDraw exceeds deck size minus a reserve of 4 cards, but schema/server logic do not enforce that invariant.
Atomic turn payload drift: the rules still promise EventLog plus TurnHash, while the shared schema/runtime use optional events plus transaction hashes and currently broadcast empty events arrays.
Cleanup-order drift: the rules say destroyed cards move to the graveyard in LIFO order, but the combat code appends discarded cards front-then-back to discardPile.

rule_vs_test_mismatches.summary: Tests confirm pass-limit forfeits at the threshold value, not only after exceeding it.
No behavioral tests were found for Special Start Mode or special_start_window_closed; the only test references keep modeSpecialStart disabled in fixture state.
No tests were found for the documented Card Scarcity Invariant even though custom match-parameter behavior is otherwise exercised.

terminology_drift.summary: The rules use graveyard, the schema uses discardPile, and the client help uses Discard Pile (GY).
The rules use EventLog and TurnHash in Section 20.2, while architecture/code use transactionLog, stateHashBefore/stateHashAfter, phaseTrace, and events.

undefined_terms.note: Used in the product name and review criteria, but not defined as a gameplay concept in README or the canonical rules glossary.
Behavior is described, but there is no broader glossary entry or architecture note explaining its lifecycle.
The rules use graveyard, but there is no canonical cross-reference to the runtime/storage term discardPile.

developer_docs.coverage: Setup prerequisites, package overview, local URLs, local observability commands.
Validation commands, workspace layout, doc pointers.
Admin auth and A/B route behavior.
UI route map and HTTP/WS surface.
Script surface for build, test, docs, QA, and deployment.
Automated drift checks for docs/FSM authority.

missing_topics.summary: No explicit contributor checklist for changing game rules across docs/RULES.md, shared schemas, state machine authority, tests, generated docs, and client help.
Docs generation and regeneration workflow is scattered; CONTRIBUTING.md does not cover pnpm docs:build, pnpm schema:gen, or scripts/docs/render-site-flow.sh.
Database setup, migrations, and persistence prerequisites are not documented as a single onboarding path.
Replay-validation and OpenAPI update workflows are not documented for contributors even though they are part of the verifiable-specification goal.
Deployment/release docs exist in scripts and plans, but not as a stable contributor/operator runbook in core docs.

gameplay_documents.coverage: Full rules text with deployment, combat, suit boundaries, and replay semantics.
Short player-facing explanations for LP, battlefield, hand, stats, and battle log.
Very short game description and pointer to the canonical rules.

missing_explanations.summary: What a phalanx is and how the board forms a phalanx is not explained for new players.
The gameplay docs do not present a dedicated beginner-facing win-conditions explanation.
There is no illustrated example turn showing deployment, attack, cleanup, reinforcement, and draw in one concrete scenario.
The player-facing docs do not bridge canonical rule terms to UI terms such as graveyard vs discard pile or lifepoints vs LP.

documentation_strengths.summary: Canonical authority is explicit and discoverable.
The repository already has a real documentation drift guard in CI.
Executable rule coverage exists for important deterministic behaviors including pass limits, face-card eligibility, replay, and phase traces.
Architecture and site-flow docs make the system surface reasonably easy to navigate for contributors.

critical_gaps.summary: Special Start Mode is documented as canonical behavior but is not implemented or behaviorally tested.
The documented event model and atomic turn payload do not match runtime behavior; events are structurally defined but not emitted, and the rules still use stale EventLog/TurnHash language.
The canonical rules still contain concrete drift in card-id format, pass-limit semantics, and card-scarcity validation.
Terminology is not normalized across canonical docs, schemas, and UI help.
The repository is below the verifiable-specification standard because docs do not yet provide a stable rule-to-engine trace matrix or end-to-end replay/event verification narrative.

recommended_improvements.summary: Reconcile docs/RULES.md Sections 2.1, 3.3, 16, 17, and 20.2 with the actual engine/server behavior or change the implementation to match the canonical text.
Either implement Special Start Mode and the documented PhalanxEvent emission model, or explicitly remove/downgrade those claims from the canonical and architecture docs.
Add a rule-to-engine traceability matrix that maps each canonical rules section to shared schema fields, engine modules, tests, and server endpoints.
Add an explicit win-conditions section and a short gameplay glossary that defines phalanx, graveyard/discard pile, LP/lifepoints, target chain, boundary, and carryover.

Extend scripts/ci/verify-doc-fsm-consistency.ts to catch capitalized legacy terms (TurnHash/EventLog), card-id format drift, pass-threshold drift, SpecialStart implementation absence, and card-scarcity validation drift.
Document contributor workflows for rule changes, generated docs refresh, replay/OpenAPI updates, and persistence/migration setup in CONTRIBUTING.md or a dedicated docs/dev guide.

documentation_health.overall_assessment: The repository already has a recognizable documentation system: a canonical rules file, a strong architecture overview, generated API docs, executable rule tests, and a CI drift check. It is not yet a verifiable specification because multiple canonical claims drift from runtime behavior, event documentation overstates what the system emits, and rule-to-engine traceability still depends on code archaeology rather than documentation.
